coredata.dc:title: “On-off-on” fluorescence switch of graphene quantum dots: A cationic control strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jie
Wang, Zhongteng
Yang, Juan
Xia, Xinming
Yi, Ruobing
Jiang, Jie
Liu, Wei
Chen, Junlang
Chen, Liang
Xu, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence switch based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is of great interest in the areas of environment, medicine and data security because of their promising applications. However, the known ternary “on-off-on” fluorescent switches of GQDs are very few due to the limited effective fluorescence recovery strategies. In this work, we proposed a new cationic control strategy and designed a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch, i.e., GQDs-Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+-Al3+/Ga3+/In3+, where the fluorescence of synthesized GQDs was quenched by Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+, and subsequently was recovered by three kinds of Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+. The corresponding recovery degree can reach up to 0.81 compared with the pristine GQDs. The XPS results combining with quantum chemical calculations illustrated that the change of π-conjugated region caused by cations dominated the quenching and recovery of fluorescence. Furthermore, based on this “on-off-on” mechanism, a kind of anti-counterfeiting stealth ink for information encryption was also suggested. This work is the first report that Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ can be used for fluorescence recovery, and the cationic control strategy proposed here provides a new and promising strategy for the development of “on-off-on” fluorescent switch in future studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
On-off-on fluorescence switch
Cationic control strategy
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The possible mechanism of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch
Theoretical calculations on the interactions between ions and GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 12074341 , 12075211 , 11975206 , 11875236 ), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20B030002), the Scientific Research and Developed Fund of Zhejiang A&F University (No. 2017FR032, 2019FR005, 2019FR006).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Corresponding PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 390 nm. Inset are photographs of the corresponding GQDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (right) (b) C1s XPS spectra of GQDs.

(a) TEM image of GQDs. (b) AFM image of GQDs with corresponding height profiles taken along the marked white line. (c) Size distribution of GQDs determined by measuring GQDs flakes on 300 × 300 nm2 AFM images.
The fluorescence intensity of 3 mL GQDs before and after being added 20 μL of 3 mol/L various metal cations solution.
(a) Schematic diagram of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. The relative fluorescence ratios of GQDs before and after being added 20 μL Al3+/Ga3+/In3+ for (b) Cu2+, (c) Fe3+, and (d) Cr3+systems.
C1s XPS spectra of (a) GQDs-Cu2+, (b) GQDs-Al3+, (c) GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+.
(a) The structure of binary complex (b) The distance (Å) and binding energies ΔE (kcal/mol) between C24 and twelve ions.
Structures and key geometrical parameters of binary complexes composed by Cu2+/ Fe3+/ Cr3+ and C24 with one epoxy or hydroxyl group.

Structures and key measurement parameters of C24-Cu2+ with/without Al3+ (a) and (b) for C24, (c) and (d) for C24-M1 model, (e) and (f) for C24-M2 model. Spheres in purple, brown, cyan, white, and red represent Al3+, Cu2+, carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, respectively.

“True password: unbelieve” written on a mixed cellulose filter membrane (MCF) using different types of invisible ink under different lighting conditions. (a) natural light, (b) UV light, and (c) after spraying Al3+ solution under UV light.

head.ce:title: “On-off-on” fluorescence switch of graphene quantum dots: A cationic control strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Optical Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Optical Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou 311300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201800, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201800, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: J. L. and Z. W. are equal first author contributors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch of graphene quantum dots by a cationic control strategy.

Fluorescence switch based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is of great interest in the areas of environment, medicine and data security because of their promising applications. However, the known ternary “on-off-on” fluorescent switches of GQDs are very few due to the limited effective fluorescence recovery strategies. In this work, we proposed a new cationic control strategy and designed a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch, i.e., GQDs-Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+-Al3+/Ga3+/In3+, where the fluorescence of synthesized GQDs was quenched by Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+, and subsequently was recovered by three kinds of Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+. The corresponding recovery degree can reach up to 0.81 compared with the pristine GQDs. The XPS results combining with quantum chemical calculations illustrated that the change of π-conjugated region caused by cations dominated the quenching and recovery of fluorescence. Furthermore, based on this “on-off-on” mechanism, a kind of anti-counterfeiting stealth ink for information encryption was also suggested. This work is the first report that Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ can be used for fluorescence recovery, and the cationic control strategy proposed here provides a new and promising strategy for the development of “on-off-on” fluorescent switch in future studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detection has been one of significant optical detection techniques in environment, medicine and data security fields, due to its operational simplicity, high sensitivity, and stability [1,2]. By controlling the fluorescence on or off, a variety of fluorescent switches have been developed to detect many metal ions and biomolecules that play the significant role in biological systems or environmental processes [3,4]. Therefore, the exploration of various effective and selective fluorescent switch combination has always attracted extensive experimental and theoretical attention [2,5–8].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [9], a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have been successfully applied to chemo-/biosensor, cell imaging, photoelectric devices, and information encryption, due to their excellent fluorescence properties, adjustable optical properties and biocompatibility [10–16]. The photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs is extremely sensitive to minute environmental perturbation and they can intimately interact with the analyte owing to their small size [8]. Hence, GQDs have been one of promising fluorescent switch materials in recent years, and various fluorescent switches based on GQDs have been fabricated [17,18]. These switches can be mainly divided into two categories, i.e., “on-off” and “on-off-on”. For “on-off” fluorescent switches, the most common is the binary combination composed by GQDs and metal cations. Many studies have shown that the fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched by metal cation, leading to the formation of “on-off” switch that can be used for the ion detection [19–21]. For example, in 2012, Wang et al. [22], first reported that GQDs could achieve the Fe3+ detection due to the selective fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ ions. Subsequently, a variety of fluorescent Fe3+ sensors based on GQDs were developed quickly and the limit of detection was continuously reduced [19,23,24]. Similarly, several studies have demonstrated that some functionalized GQDs, such as heteroatom doped GQDs and L-cystine-modified GQDs, can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the Cu2+ [3,25] . In addition, besides the detection of single ions, parallel detection of multiple ions in ion mixture samples also can be achieved forming a multi-“on-off” switch [3,22,26]. In these studies involving “on-off” switch, several possible quenching mechanisms caused by metal cations also have been suggested, such as intramolecular and intermolecular charge transformation, fluorescence resonance energy transfer and aggregation [27,28].

In contrast to the first category, the known ternary “on-off-on” fluorescent switches are very limited [2,8]. The “on-off-on” switch can be seen as the extension of “on-off” switch. A fluorescence recovery process is further needed after fluorescence quenching. Hence, it is extremely important and challenging to explore the appropriate and effective fluorescence recovery strategy in the development of “on-off-on” fluorescent switch. To our best knowledge, there are only two strategies in current studies. The first one is to use biomolecules as chelators to remove metal cation from GQDs surfaces, which benefits from the higher binding affinity of cation with biomolecules than with GQDs [3,19,22]. For example, some studies have showed that cysteine could restore the fluorescence after Cu2+/Ag+/Fe3+/Hg2+ ions quenching due to the formation of stable chelates [3,25,29,30], and a corresponding cysteine fluorescence sensor was established according to this switching mechanism [9]. The second known strategy of fluorescence recovery is to utilize fluorescence differences between GQDs and different metal cations, where these metal cations are the same element but in different valence states. The only case is Fe3+ and Fe2+ combination. Experimental results show that Fe3+ can quench the fluorescence of GQDs while Fe2+ has no effect [3]. Therefore, when an oxidant or reductant was used, the fluorescence quenching or recovery could be achieved. Using this principle, Chen et al. achieved the examination of the cellular dynamic activity for the first time [31]. Clearly, known fluorescence recovery strategies are very few, which obviously limits the development of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. Is there other probable strategy to recover the quenched fluorescence?

In this work, we proposed a cationic control strategy to design a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch of GQDs, where the “off” and “on” states are both controlled using only single cations. Experimental results showed that the fluorescence of prepared green and pollution-free GQDs using starch could be quenched by Fe3+, Cu2+ and Cr3+, and further recovered by Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+, forming a novel “on-off-on” fluorescent switch. The “on-off-on” mechanism was speculated through the change of C1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and the possible interactions between GQDs and ions were investigated using quantum chemical calculations. This work is the first report that Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ can be used to recover fluorescence, and the cationic control strategy proposed here presents a new strategy to the “on-off-on” fluorescent switch in future studies.

All chemical supplies NaCl, KCl, LiCl·H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, GaCl3 and InCl3·4H2O used in our work are analytical reagents purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. and can be used without further processing. Aqueous chloride solutions of various salts were prepared from ultra-pure water with a conductivity of 18.2 MΩ.

To synthesize GQDs, 1.8 g of starch powder was added into 150 mL Deionized water and mixed with a magnetic stirrer at 333 K for 15 min. Then, the uniform solution was further heated at 463 K for 2 h in an autoclave. Next, a pale-yellow suspension was obtained and centrifuge at 15,000 rpm for 20 min to remove brown impurities. The concentration of the as-prepared GQDs suspension was about 12 mg/mL.

In a typical experiment, 20 μL chloride solutions (3 mol/L) of Na+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ were added into 3 mL of GQDs solutions and sonicated the mixed solutions for a time, respectively. The fluorescent spectrum and corresponding fluorescent intensity of the mixed solutions and pure GQDs solution were detected by fluorescence spectrometer. The relative fluorescence ratio (I/I0) is used to evaluate the fluorescence quenching and re-brightening efficiency, where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs solution before and after being added metal ions, respectively. The fluorescent brightness of these solution before and after being added salt solution were observed under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm center wavelength) and have been documented by photographs.

Photographs of the GQDs under ultraviolet light with 365 nm excitation were obtained by a UV analyzer (ZF-7 N, Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd.). All fluorescence spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hong Kong Tian mei Co., Ltd.). The XPS results of freeze-dried GQDs powders were performed via a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The GQDs powders were freeze-dried by a gland vacuum freeze dryer (LGJ-10, Beijing Songyuan Huaxing Co., Ltd.). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) results were collected using Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. GQD flakes were measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Multimode-8) and the transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Talos F200C).

To understand the possible interactions between GQDs and each cation, and the effect of Group IIIA ions at the molecular level, we performed the quantum chemical calculations for the binary (GQDs-cation) and ternary (GQDs-ion-Group IIIA cation) complexes that involved the same components with the experiment. Here, the C24H12 hydrocarbons (C24) with/without oxygen-containing functional groups were chosen as the model for GQDs unoxidized and oxidized regions, which has been successfully used to study GQDs in previous studies [32,33]. For unoxidized regions, we totally considered three adsorption sites of ion on the surface of GQDs, i.e., the top of one C atom, the bridge of two C-atoms, and the center of the ring (see Fig. S1). For oxidized regions, four kinds of oxygen-containing functional groups, i.e., epoxy (O), hydroxyl(OH), carbonyl (CO), and carboxyl (COOH) were involved, among which epoxy (O) and hydroxyl(OH) groups can exist on the surface. In addition, we also designed two models, i.e., M1 and M2, which have similar oxygen context, ratios of oxygen-containing functional groups, and absorption maximum wavelengths with experimental data (see Fig. S2 and Table S1). All the structural optimization and frequency calculations were carried out using the hybrid density functional B3LYP [34–36]/6-31G(d) [37], which has been proven that it is reliable to study the graphene system [38]. In order to avoid the influence of model size, a larger model (C54H18) was further employed and the hybrid density functional with dispersion correction [39] was used. Note that def2-SVP [40] basis set was used for In3+ system. The obtained local minima have no imaginary frequencies, and all the energies were corrected by the zero-point energy (ZPVE). Partial charges (δ) were calculated using natural bond orbital (NBO) analysis [41]. For the excitation transitions of GQDs, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) [42] was employed, which has been demonstrated that the calculated absorption spectra are in good agreement with experimental spectra [43–45]. Due to the experiment in aqueous solution, the solvent (water) was included based on PCM [46] in the TDDFT calculations, and a minimum of the first 25 lowest excited states were calculated. All the electronic calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 programs package [47]. The visualizations of structures and molecular orbitals were done using CYLview [48] and Multiwfn [49].

According to abundant research on GQDs, we chose edible starch as the precursor to prepare GQDs using a green and effective one-pot hydrothermal method. Previous study has demonstrated that this synthetic method has many advantages including green and low-cost raw materials, no impurity, simple operation and no complicated post-processing steps [50]. As shown Fig. 1 a, the synthesized GQDs solution is transparent pale-yellow liquid under visible light indicating a good dispersibility, and exhibits the PL blue emission under 365 nm ultraviolet light. We further tested the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 390 nm and showed the corresponding fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1a. Results show that the emission peak gradually red shifts with the increase of the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, the maximum emission peak is obtained at the wavelength of 440 nm. It suggests that the PL of the GQDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength, and such excitation-dependent behavior can be explained by a wide distribution of particle size [51] and surface defects of GQDs [52–54]. The weak emission peak of 494 nm in Fig. 1a, is independence of the excitation wavelength. The emission wavelength of 494 nm in the GQDs is due to the formation of molecular states containing double bonded oxygen functional groups (CO or COOH) on the GQDs [55]. Therefore, in the following fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments, 360 nm is chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength.

The compositions and contents of prepared GQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S3 shows the total XPS survey spectrum of GQDs containing only two distinct peaks of C1s (286.08 eV) and O1s (532.08 eV). The atomic concentrations of C and O in GQDs are 67.8% and 32.2%, respectively, which is consistent with early reports [50]. As shown in Fig. 1b, the C1s spectra can be decomposed into four peaks, corresponding to different functional groups, i.e., CC/CC at 284.47 eV, CO at 285.98 eV, CO at 287.27 eV and OCO at 288.48 eV, showing the aromatic ring structure together with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs.

The surface morphology and size distribution of synthesized GQDs were evaluated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements. The TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a, and the GQDs have a particle size distribution of 1–25 nm according to the TEM image analysis. This was further confirmed using AFM measurements (see Fig. 2b and c). The AFM image reveals that the main particle sizes are distributed in the range of 5–15 nm, and the corresponding total frequencies are 76.6%. Note that there are some large particles with a size of >25 nm, which may be impurity of carbide precipitate.In addition,the thickness of the GQDs is ~0.5 nm or ~1.0 nm (Fig. 2b inset), suggesting that the GQDs are single-layered or double-layered, which is consistent with the typical thickness of the GQDs sheets [50].

Previous studies have shown that the PL of GQDs is sensitive to many metal cations, and some heavy metal cations can selectively quench fluorescence [4,19,28,56], which is usually used as the first step of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. In this work, twelve kinds of metal cations, i.e., Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, In3+ and Ga3+, were chosen to study the fluorescence effect on GQDs. The fluorescence intensity changes were summarized when each kind of metal salt cations (4–20 μl, 3 mol/L) was gradually added to 3 mL of GQDs solution. Among cations considered here, 20 μl of 3 mol/L Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ could clearly quench the prepared GQDs, and the fluorescence intensity clearly decreased from the initial 2300 a.u. to 755 a.u. for Cu2+, 642 a.u. for Fe3+ and 674 a.u. for Cr3+, respectively (see Fig. 3 ). The details of quenching degree with the cation amounts are shown in Figs. S4–S6. For the rest of cations, their effects on fluorescence intensity are very small and Ga3+ can enhance slightly. Moreover, we examined the fluorescence stability before adding ions. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs are nearly unchanged within 7 h. This indirectly implies that the fluorescence intensity changes should be caused by the addition of cations. The possible quenched mechanism and interaction between GQDs and cations will be discussed combining with quantum chemical calculations in the following. In all, the fluorescence of GQDs synthesized here can be quenched by Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+, which will be used as the first step of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch.

After realizing the fluorescence quenching, it is significant to recover the fluorescence that have been quenched by Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+. According to our previous studies on cation-π [57], we first proposed a cationic control fluorescence recovery strategy in this work. In order to verify this assumption, we totally considered nine kinds of cations that have no effect on PL in the first step, i.e., Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, In3+ and Ga3+, and 20 μl of each cation was added to the solution of GQDs-Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+, respectively. Inspiringly, we finally found that three Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ could successfully recover the brightness of GQDs (see Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence ratio changes for recovered systems are shown in Fig. 4b–d, and the details of recovery degree with the cation amounts are shown in Figs. S4–S6. As shown in Fig. 4b–d, after adding Group IIIA ions, the relative fluorescence ratios increased significantly. Among these three cations, Ga3+ had the best effect to recover fluorescence intensities, which were recovered to 0.81, 0.67, and 0.70 for Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ system, respectively. In all, these experimental results illustrated that three Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ all can recover the fluorescence that have been quenched by Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, which is also the first report that fluorescence quenching and recovery were achieved using only single cations. Hence, the cationic control fluorescence recovery strategy proposed here is feasible and this ternary combination, i.e., GQDs-Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+-Al3+/Ga3+/In3+, could be a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. The possible mechanism will be discussed in the following combining with quantum chemical calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch
ce:section.ce:para: XPS results could provide the distribution of each component when ions were added to GQDs, which may be useful to understand the possible mechanism of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. Here, we took GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ as the example. The XPS spectra of GQDs-Cu2+, GQDs-Al3+, and GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ are shown in Fig. 5 . First, by comparing XPS spectra of GQDs (Fig. 1b) and GQDs-Cu2+ (Fig. 5a), we found that the main difference between them was the content of CC/CC, which was 23.54% in GQDs-Cu2+ and 32.92% in GQDs. This decrease indicated that the effective aromatic rings became less in GQDs-Cu2+. This is supported by our previous work that cations can greatly affect the conjugated double bonds of aromatic groups in GQDs due to the existence of the strong cation - π interaction [57,58]. Since the π-conjugated region of GQDs surfaces dominated the PL of GQDs [59], we speculated that the quenched fluorescence was mainly caused by the decrease of π-conjugated region that can contribute to PL. This assumption also can be used to explain the results of GQDs-Al3+. As shown in Fig. 5b, the content of CC/CC in GQDs-Al3+ is nearly unchanged (34.34%), which is consistent with the fact that the fluorescence intensity was not affected when Al3+ was added (see Fig. 3). However, it is interesting to find that CC/CC content of GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ is 33.84%, which is nearly twice of that in GQDs-Cu2+. This means that the existence of Al3+ in GQDs-Cu2+ could recover the π-conjugated region, and this also should be the main reason why Al3+ could recover the fluorescence of GQDs-Cu2+. It is worth noting that the content of oxygen groups in GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ were reduced compared with GQDs-Cu2+. It is clear that cations also have the effect on oxidized regions in GQDs. These XPS results are also supported by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis. As shown in Fig. S8, the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-Cu2+ changed significantly compared with that of GQDs, while the existence of Al3+ could recover the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-Cu2+ to similar to that of pure GQDs. The interactions between cations and un-oxidized/oxidized regions in GQDs were further investigated using quantum chemical calculations. Moreover, our designed novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch in this work is universal and suitable for the GQDs prepared by various methods, such as nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and hydroxylated GQDs (OH-GQDs), as shown in Fig. S9

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations on the interactions between ions and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the interactions between GQDs and cations at the molecular level, we theoretically calculated the corresponding the binary and ternary complexes that involved the same components with experiments. The surface of GQDs usually can be divided into two regions, i.e., unoxidized and oxidized regions, where the latter has an important influence on the ultraviolet–visible absorption peak position [38]. Hence, the interactions between cation and two regional models were both investigated in this work. Firstly, for the unoxidized region, the binary C24-ion complexes for twelve ions were calculated at the level of B3LYP/6-31G(d). Calculations results show that all the ions except Al3+, Ga3+ and In3+ can be adsorbed on C24, and the structures that ion lies on the center of the ring (see Fig. 6 a) have the lowest energy in each system. Note that all the systems in low-spin state and high-spin state were both calculated. Except Fe3+ and Cr3+ system (in quartet state), the most stable isomers are in low-spin state. The distance (Å) and binding energies ΔE (kcal/mol) between C24 and each ion are shown in Fig. 6b. Among these ions, the transition metal ions have the shorter distances and larger binding energies with C24 than that of main group ions, indicating a stronger interaction. For Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ cations that can quench the fluorescence in the experiment, the distances between C24 and these three ions are 1.62 Å, 1.72 Å and 2.04 Å, and the corresponding binding energies are −289.67, −693.52 and −636.35 kcal/mol, respectively. Partial charges of metal ions in complexes are 0.55 e for Cu2+, 2.62 e for Fe3+, and 2.91 e for Cr3+. It is clear that there are charge transformations between metal cations and C24. This is also supported by the molecular orbitals of these binary complexes that the obvious overlap can be found between the d-orbitals of cations and the delocalized π-orbitals of C24. (see Fig. S10) Hence, the strong adsorption of Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+ affected the conjugated π on the GQDs surface, which is consistent with experimental XPS results that GQDs-Cu2+ has low CC/CC content. These results are also similar with previous some studies on mental ions and GQDs that the strong adsorption with the charge transformation caused the fluorescence quenching [60]. Because Group IIIA ions cannot be adsorbed on current model, in order to avoid the effect of model size, we took Al3+ as the example to study its interaction with C54H18 and the corresponding Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ calculations were as the references. Results showed that Al3+ could stably adsorb on C54 with 699.89 kcal/mol binding energies. For Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, binding energies of them with C54 are slightly larger (14–17%) than that with C24. This is reasonable because with the increase of the number of conjugated aromatic rings, the cation-π interaction between the ions and the model increases, resulting in higher adsorption energies. Additionally, the charge changes in C54 system are nearly the same with that in C24 system. Although Al3+ or Zn2+ have very considerable cation-π interactions, their completely filled p shell in Al3+ and d shell in Zn2+ make them difficult for electron transfer between GQDs and cations, thus no significant effect on the PL of GQDs was observed in experiments (see Fig. 3).

In order to investigate the possible reactions of Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ on the oxidized region, we first took C24 with one epoxy or one hydroxyl as the model. For epoxy, Cu2+ and Fe3+ broke one O-C bond between epoxy and C24, and inserted it forming one O-Cu/Fe bond (see Fig. 7 a and c). While Cr3+ totally reduced the epoxy, where Cr3+ instead of C24 binds to O-atom (see Fig. 7d). For hydroxyl, Cu2+ directly bind on the bridge site on C24 and had a weak interaction with hydroxyl (see Fig. 7b), while Fe3+ and Cr3+ both replaced hydroxyl group to interact with C24 (see Fig. 7e and f). In order to approximate the oxidized region under true condition, we further designed two models C24-M1 and C24-M2, and took Cu2+ as the example. As shown in Fig. 7c and e, the binding formation of Cu2+ on the surface of these two models is similar with the above discussions of C24 model. In all, for oxidized region, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ ions all can adsorbed stably in the oxidized region through forming covalent bonds with oxygen-containing functional groups.

Based on the discussion above, calculations on the interactions between cations and unoxidized/oxidized regions in binary complexes are useful to understand why the fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched by Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+ on the experiments. However, in order to reveal why Al3+/Ga3+/In3+ can recover the quenched fluorescence, the calculations on ternary complex are necessary. Here, we chose GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ combination as the example and took the calculations on C24, C24-M1 and C24-M2 models. The structures with and without Al3+ are shown in Fig. 8 . Calculation results showed that the presence of Al3+ in all models can lead to the removal of Cu2+ from the aromatic region, resulting in the recovery of the π-conjugated region on the GQDs surface. This is consistent with the XPS results that CC/CC content in GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ is higher than that in GQDs-Cu2+, which causes the re-brightness of fluorescence. In addition, Al3+ also can react with Cu2+ in oxidized region and form the Al-O-Cu fragment, leading to reduce the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. This may be the reason that why contents of oxygen groups in GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ were lower than that in GQDs-Cu2+. In all, consistent with experimental analysis, theoretical calculations also imply that Al3+ can recover the π-conjugated region both in unoxidized and oxidized regions of the GQDs-Cu2+ surface.

The “on-off-on” fluorescence switches based on GQDs and metal cations not only can achieve the detection of ions in the environment, but also have great potential applications in information security encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications [11,61]. Here, we took GQDs-Cu2+-Al3+ system as the example to prepare an anti-counterfeiting stealth ink, and chose 360 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength. A mixed cellulose filter membrane (MCF) membrane was perpared, and the words “Ture password: unbelieve” were written on the membrane, where “Ture” and “un” were written in GQDs-Cu2+ solution and “password: believe” were written in GQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 9 a, when the MCF film is illuminated by natural light, no text can be seen. When the MCF film is irradiated with ultraviolet light (365 nm), the words “password: believe” will appear (Fig. 9b). After further spraying Al3+ solution on the surface of the MCF film, the words “Ture password: unbelieve” (Fig. 9c) appear immediately, achieving the effect of information encryption. Clearly, GQDs-Cu2+ with and without Al3+ can be used as a novel anti-counterfeiting stealth ink to protect important information. Hence, this kind of “on-off-on” fluorescence switch mechanism reported here may have the promising applications in the field of information security encryption and decryption.

In this work, we proposed a cationic control fluorescence recovery strategy, and designed a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch based on GQDs. Experimental results showed that the synthesized green and pollution-free GQDs using starch could be quenched by Fe3+, Cu2+, and Cr3+, and three kinds of Group IIIA ions Al3+, Ga3+, In3+ could recover the quenched fluorescence. The XPS results combining with quantum chemical calculations revealed that the change of π-conjugated region caused by cations dominated the quenching and recovery of fluorescence. This work is the first time to achieve the fluorescence recovery using Al3+/Ga3+/In3+ cations, and this ternary combination, i.e., GQDs-Cu2+/Fe3+/Cr3+-Al3+/Ga3+/In3+ represents a novel “on-off-on” fluorescence switch. Furthermore, this is extended to prepare an anti-counterfeiting stealth ink for the information encryption. This work provides a new fluorescence recovery strategy to develop the “on-off-on” fluorescent switch in future studies and may have the potential applications in the fields of metal ion detection and information encryption.

Jie Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Zhongteng Wang: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Juan Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Xinming Xia: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Ruobing Yi: Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation. Jie Jiang: Resources, Supervision, Validation. Wei Liu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Junlang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Liang Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Jing Xu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12074341, 12075211, 11975206, 11875236), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20B030002), the Scientific Research and Developed Fund of Zhejiang A&F University (No. 2017FR032, 2019FR005, 2019FR006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.149110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
